title: E-Commerce Bot Detection
spl:
index=web sourcetype="nginx:access" uri="/checkout*"
| eval bot_score=if(match(http_user_agent,"(?i)(bot|crawler|python)"),1,0)
| stats count, dc(session_id) as sess, avg(request_time_ms) as avg_rt by src
| where count>200 AND sess<5 AND avg_rt<50
| sort -count
description: Protects inventory, loyalty points, and checkout from scalping and credential stuffing.
value: Protects inventory, loyalty points, and checkout from scalping and credential stuffing.
implementation: Layer bot management (JS challenge, CAPTCHA); rate limit by ASN and fingerprint.
visualization: Table (suspicious IPs), Line chart (bot traffic share), Map (ASN concentration).
knownFalsePositives: Bot-like traffic from price aggregators, good bots (SEO, partners), and mobile app retries; use WAF and ASN allowlists to reduce false hunts.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: CDN/WAF, application logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="nginx:access"`, `sourcetype="akamai:siem"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Layer bot management (JS challenge, CAPTCHA); rate limit by ASN and fingerprint.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=web sourcetype="nginx:access" uri="/checkout*"
| eval bot_score=if(match(http_user_agent,"(?i)(bot|crawler|python)"),1,0)
| stats count, dc(session_id) as sess, avg(request_time_ms) as avg_rt by src
| where count>200 AND sess<5 AND avg_rt<50
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**E-Commerce Bot Detection** — Protects inventory, loyalty points, and checkout from scalping and credential stuffing.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="nginx:access"`, `sourcetype="akamai:siem"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): CDN/WAF, application logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: web; **sourcetype**: nginx:access. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=web, sourcetype="nginx:access". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **bot_score** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count>200 AND sess<5 AND avg_rt<50` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (suspicious IPs), Line chart (bot traffic share), Map (ASN concentration).
grandmaExplanation: In Retail and E-Commerce, we pay close attention to "E-Commerce Bot Detection" in our day-to-day work so that fraud, waste, and compliance issues do not slip by quietly. We help teams focus on the few situations that need a person, not every routine blip in the data.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable for the primary search: it relies on application- and industry-specific log formats, not a normalized CIM data model. Optional mapping to CIM, if you adopt it, is a separate follow-on project.
